Salmon Nation

type
organization
location_lat
45.5
background_section
## Background For thousands of years, the salmon's annual return defined the rhythm of life for the Indigenous peoples of North America, who developed rich cultural traditions around this natural abundance. This place—where salmon and human communities have been intertwined for millennia—represents a unique bioregion now known as Salmon Nation. Today, [30 million people live within the historical range of wild Pacific salmon](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=irroTN2FSFk), citizens of this nation whether they realize it or not. They inherit the benefits of an ecosystem that, when healthy, sustains not just salmon but entire watersheds and the communities that depend on them. The underlying principle is elegantly simple yet profound: healthy runs of wild Pacific salmon, as they journey from mountain streams to ocean and back again, connect entire watersheds and serve as a comprehensive indicator of bioregional health. When human systems—agriculture, forestry, utilities, transportation—align with natural systems, salmon flourish. And when salmon flourish, so do we. Salmon represent what one might call a [Nature State](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=irroTN2FSFk) as distinct from nation-states—a place defined by natural systems that has always existed, transcending political boundaries. The salmon's return represents the greatest nutrient transfer between habitats on Earth, nourishing forests, entire food webs, and future generations. This principle has proven transformative: in the Okanagan region, a remarkable alliance including three First Nations has revitalized sockeye salmon populations from a mere [3,000 fish 25 years ago to 800,000 in 2024](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=irroTN2FSFk). These returning salmon now represent [70% of the sockeye in the once-mighty Columbia River basin](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=irroTN2FSFk), simultaneously restoring ecological function and cultural connections. ### Origins and Network The concept of Salmon Nation emerged from three decades of conservation work at [Ecotrust](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=irroTN2FSFk), founded by Spencer Beebe in 1991. Through work with First Nations and rural communities across the bioregion, Ecotrust developed deep relationships and insights about bioregional systems thinking. A formative early project involved the Haisla First Nation's protection of the Kitlope River Watershed—nearly a million acres protected through the first co-management agreement in British Columbia, an experience that shaped understanding of the inseparability of ecological protection, cultural revival, and community well-being. In 2019, this vision crystallized into Salmon Nation Trust, a public benefit LLC designed to invest in bioregional institutional infrastructure supporting regenerative development. Governed by public-benefit-oriented Trustees, the Trust channels values-aligned capital from philanthropic and mission-driven investors who do not expect financial return. The Trust serves as the foundational organization for the entire Salmon Nation ecosystem, incubating and spawning bioregional ventures while operating on the principle that healthy ecosystems and thriving communities are inseparable. Throughout the bioregion, Salmon Nation works with what they call [edge communities](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=irroTN2FSFk)—smaller communities, Indigenous communities, and rural communities at the edges of modern society. In these places, they find the Ravens– smart, tough, tenacious individuals who have said, "That old industrial system doesn't work for me, and I'm going to do it differently." This philosophy recognizes that in moments of disequilibrium, it's often those at the margins who demonstrate the most innovative approaches to regenerative living.

temp
improper location but good for now?
description
A network of public benefit companies whose purpose is to inspire, enable, and invest in regenerative development across the Salmon Nation bioregion.
id
salmon-nation
vision_legacy_section
## Vision and Legacy Salmon Nation is a movement built on the recognition that when salmon flourish, so do we. Through Storytelling of Ravens, Magic Canoe, Edge Prize, and Salmon Returns, Salmon Nation continues to weave together a network of changemakers, flow capital to regeneration, and demonstrate that finance can be an act of cultural and ecological healing. As the network learns to "paddle together" in the magic canoe, it offers a model for how bioregional organizations can incubate, spawn, and support regenerative transformation.
website
https://www.salmonnation.net/
bioregional_context
The Salmon Nation bioregion spans a vast territory defined by the traditional range of wild Pacific salmon in North America, stretching from the Salinas River in California northward to the North Slopes of Alaska, unified by salmon watersheds.
category
Bioregional Organizer
location_lng
